A React component re-renders when its parent re-renders because **that's React's default behavior** — when a component renders, it recursively re-renders *all* of its children, regardless of whether their props changed. This is unrelated to props being "different"; it's just how the render tree propagates. Here are the common causes and fixes: ## 1. It's just the default (most common) By default, a child re-renders whenever the parent does. To opt out, wrap the child in `React.memo`, which makes it re-render only when its props actually change: ```jsx const Child = React.memo(function Child({ value }) { return
{value}
; }); ``` ## 2. `React.memo` isn't helping because props are new every render `React.memo` does a *shallow* comparison. If the parent passes a new object, array, or function each render, the reference differs every time and memo is defeated: ```jsx // ❌ New object/function each render → memo useless doThing()} /> ``` Fix with `useMemo` / `useCallback`: ```jsx const style = useMemo(() => ({ color: 'red' }), []); const onClick = useCallback(() => doThing(), []); ``` ## 3. Passing `children` or non-primitive props inline Same shallow-comparison issue — inline JSX children or objects created in the parent's body are fresh references each time. ## Quick diagnostic - Is the re-render actually a *problem*? Re-rendering is cheap; only optimize if you've measured a real cost. Don't reach for `memo` everywhere. - Use the **React DevTools Profiler** ("Highlight updates when components render") to see what's re-rendering and why. - In React 19, the **React Compiler** auto-memoizes much of this, so manual `memo`/`useMemo`/`useCallback` becomes largely unnecessary. --- If you paste the actual component and its parent, I can point to the specific cause rather than the general ones.

Your component re-renders because **React re-renders all children by default when a parent re-renders** — regardless of whether that child's props actually changed. This is expected behavior, not a bug. Re-rendering isn't inherently bad (React diffs the virtual DOM and often skips actual DOM updates), but if the render is expensive or causing problems, here's how to control it. ## The usual causes & fixes **1. The child isn't memoized** By default a child re-renders whenever the parent does. Wrap it in `React.memo` so it only re-renders when its props actually change: ```jsx const Child = React.memo(function Child({ name }) { return
{name}
; }); ``` **2. You're passing a new object/array/function every render** `React.memo` does a *shallow* comparison. If you pass an inline object or function, it's a brand-new reference each render, so memo never helps: ```jsx // ❌ new references every render → memo is defeated doThing()} /> // ✅ stable references const style = useMemo(() => ({ color: 'red' }), []); const onClick = useCallback(() => doThing(), []); ``` **3. `children` or context is the culprit** - If the parent passes `children`, those elements are recreated each render. - If the child consumes a context whose value changes (or is an inline object), it re-renders regardless of memo. ## How to diagnose - **React DevTools → Profiler**, enable "Highlight updates when components render" to see what's re-rendering. - DevTools also shows **"Why did this render?"** when you record a profile. ## Before you optimize Ask whether the re-render actually costs anything. Premature `memo`/`useCallback`/`useMemo` everywhere adds complexity and its own overhead. Reach for them when you've measured a real performance problem or when a child is genuinely expensive to render. If you share the actual component + parent code, I can point to the specific reference that's breaking memoization.

Default React behavior: parent re-renders → all children re-render, regardless of whether props changed. Not a bug.

**Fix — only if re-render is actually costly** (measure first; most are cheap):

- `React.memo(Child)` — skips re-render when props shallow-equal previous. But breaks when you pass **new references each render**:
  - inline objects/arrays: `style={{color:'red'}}` → new object every time → memo useless. Hoist or `useMemo`.
  - inline callbacks: `onClick={() => …}` → new fn every time → wrap in `useCallback`.
  - `children` prop → new element each render; memo won't help.

- **Move state down**: if only part of the tree needs the changing state, push state into that subtree so the parent doesn't re-render.
- **Lift content up / pass as children**: `<Parent><ExpensiveChild/></Parent>` — `ExpensiveChild` created in grandparent doesn't re-render when `Parent`'s own state changes (same-element bailout).

Decision path:
- props genuinely change every update → re-render is correct, memo won't stop it.
- props stable but child re-renders anyway → `memo` + `useCallback`/`useMemo` on the passed refs.
- unrelated parent state forcing it → move state down or restructure with `children`.

Don't add `memo` everywhere — shallow-compare has its own cost; apply to demonstrably expensive subtrees only.

Paste the parent + child + how props are passed → exact fix.

Default React behavior: a parent re-render re-renders **all** its children, regardless of whether props changed. Nothing is "wrong" — that's the model. Reference equality, not value equality, drives it.

Common causes when you didn't expect it:

- **No memo boundary** — child isn't wrapped in `React.memo`, so it always re-renders with the parent.
- **New reference every render** — even with `React.memo`, passing an inline object/array/function (`style={{...}}`, `onClick={() => ...}`, `data={[...]}`) creates a fresh reference each parent render → memo's shallow compare fails → child re-renders anyway.
- **`children` / non-primitive props** — same reference problem.
- **Context** — a consumer re-renders on every provider value change; inline provider `value={{...}}` defeats it.

Fix, in order:

1. Wrap the child: `const Child = React.memo(Child)`.
2. Stabilize every non-primitive prop: `useCallback` for handlers, `useMemo` for objects/arrays.
   ```jsx
   const handleClick = useCallback(() => {...}, [deps]);
   const data = useMemo(() => [...], [deps]);
   ```
3. Both are required together — `memo` without stable props does nothing.

Verify: React DevTools Profiler → "Highlight updates" or "Why did this render". Don't optimize blindly — a re-render is only a problem if it's measurably expensive; most are cheaper than the memoization overhead.

One caveat cutting the other way — memo helps only when the child is expensive *and* re-renders often with identical props; otherwise it's net cost.

Paste the parent + child + how the child's props are built → I'll point at the exact offending line.

Default React behavior: parent re-renders → **all** children re-render, regardless of whether their props changed. React doesn't diff props to decide *whether* to run the child; it re-runs the child then diffs the output. Nothing wrong by default — usually cheap.

Fix only if profiling shows it matters:

**1. Memoize the child** — `React.memo` skips re-render when props are shallow-equal.
```jsx
const Child = React.memo(function Child(props) { ... })
```

**2. Then stabilize the props** — `memo` is defeated by new references each render:
- Inline objects/arrays: `<Child style={{color:'red'}}/>` → new object every time. Hoist or `useMemo`.
- Callbacks: `onClick={() => ...}` → new function every time. `useCallback`.
- Children as JSX passed in props → same issue.

**3. Or don't lift the render at all** — move the frequently-changing state *down* into the component that uses it, or pass expensive subtrees as `children`/props from higher up so they aren't re-created by the updating parent.

Key distinction: re-render ≠ DOM update. React re-running your function is cheap; only committed DOM diffs touch the screen. Reach for `memo`/`useMemo`/`useCallback` when the Profiler flags a real cost — premature memoization adds its own overhead and complexity.

To confirm which reason applies: React DevTools Profiler → "why did this render" shows props vs. parent-render as the trigger.

Default React behavior: parent re-renders → **all** children re-render, regardless of prop changes. Not a bug — React re-renders the subtree by default.

Common causes it's *actually* a problem:

1. **Not memoized.** Wrap child in `React.memo` → skips re-render when props shallow-equal previous.
2. **New reference props every render.** `React.memo` defeated when parent passes fresh `{}`, `[]`, or inline `() => {}` — new identity each render. Fix: `useMemo`/`useCallback` in parent.
3. **New children/JSX prop.** Same reference problem for `children` or element props.
4. **Context.** Consumer re-renders on every provider value change; unstable `value={{...}}` → wrap in `useMemo`.

Minimal fix:
```jsx
const Child = React.memo(function Child({ onClick, items }) { ... });

// parent
const items = useMemo(() => [...], [deps]);
const onClick = useCallback(() => {...}, [deps]);
```
Both `memo` + stable props required — either alone won't stop it.

Diagnose which: React DevTools Profiler → "why did this render" shows changed props. Or check if props are objects/functions created inline in parent.

Note: re-render ≠ DOM update. React still diffs; unnecessary re-renders only matter if the subtree is expensive or renders are frequent. Measure before memoizing everything.

Database connection pooling is a technique for reusing a set of pre-established database connections instead of opening and closing a new one for every request.

## The problem it solves

Opening a database connection is expensive. Each new connection typically requires:

- A TCP handshake (and often TLS negotiation)
- Authentication and authorization
- Session/backend process setup on the database server
- Memory allocation on both client and server

This can take tens to hundreds of milliseconds — often far longer than the actual query. Under load, repeatedly creating and tearing down connections wastes resources and can exhaust the database's connection limit.

## How a pool works

1. On startup, the pool opens a number of connections and keeps them **idle and ready**.
2. When your code needs the database, it **borrows** (checks out) a connection from the pool.
3. It runs its queries.
4. When done, it **returns** the connection to the pool instead of closing it — so the next request reuses it.

If all connections are busy, a new request either waits for one to free up (up to a timeout) or, if configured, the pool opens an additional connection up to a maximum.

```
Requests ──▶  [ borrow ]  ──▶  Pool: [conn1][conn2][conn3]...[connN]
          ◀── [ return ] ◀──         (reused, not reopened)
```

## Key configuration knobs

| Setting | What it controls |
|---|---|
| **Min / initial size** | Connections kept warm even when idle |
| **Max size** | Upper cap on concurrent connections (protects the DB) |
| **Acquire/wait timeout** | How long a request waits for a free connection before failing |
| **Idle timeout** | Close excess idle connections to free resources |
| **Max lifetime** | Retire and replace old connections (avoids stale/leaked state) |
| **Validation / health check** | Test a connection before handing it out (e.g. a `SELECT 1`) |

## Benefits

- **Lower latency** — skip the connection setup cost per request
- **Throughput** — handle more requests with fewer resources
- **Stability** — the max size caps load on the database, preventing "too many connections" errors
- **Predictability** — bounded, controlled resource usage

## Things to watch out for

- **Sizing:** Bigger is not better. A pool larger than the database can handle causes contention. A common starting heuristic is roughly `connections ≈ (core_count × 2) + effective_spindle_count`, then tune with metrics.
- **Connection leaks:** If code borrows but forgets to return (e.g. missing a `finally`/`using` block), the pool drains and requests hang. Use max-lifetime and leak-detection settings as safety nets.
- **Stale connections:** A connection can be silently dropped by the DB, a firewall, or a load balancer. Validation queries or max-lifetime prevent handing out dead connections.
- **Serverless / many instances:** Each app instance has its own pool, so `instances × max_pool_size` can overwhelm the DB. This is why external poolers exist.

## Where pooling lives

- **In-process (library):** HikariCP (Java), `pgbouncer`-free apps using SQLAlchemy's `QueuePool` (Python), `pg-pool` (Node), `database/sql` (Go, built in).
- **External proxy/middleware:** **PgBouncer** or **Pgpool-II** for PostgreSQL, **ProxySQL** for MySQL. These sit between your app and the DB and are essential when you have many app instances (e.g. serverless functions) that would otherwise each open their own pool.

**In short:** connection pooling trades a small amount of idle resource (warm connections) for a large reduction in per-request overhead and a hard cap on database load — one of the highest-impact optimizations for any database-backed application.

Database connection pooling is a technique for managing and reusing database connections so that applications don't pay the cost of opening a new connection for every query.

## The problem it solves

Opening a database connection is expensive. Each new connection typically requires:

- A TCP handshake (and often a TLS handshake)
- Authentication (verifying username/password)
- Session and memory allocation on the database server

This can take tens to hundreds of milliseconds. If your app opens and closes a connection for every request, that overhead dominates, and under load you can exhaust the database's connection limit entirely.

## How pooling works

A connection pool is a cache of open, ready-to-use connections maintained by your application (or a middleware layer).

1. **On startup**, the pool opens a set of connections and keeps them idle.
2. **When code needs a connection**, it *borrows* one from the pool instead of creating a new one.
3. **When done**, the code *returns* the connection to the pool rather than closing it.
4. The connection stays open, ready for the next borrower.

```
App threads          Pool                 Database
  [T1] ──borrow──►  [ conn1 ] ──────────►  ┌────────┐
  [T2] ──borrow──►  [ conn2 ] ──────────►  │   DB   │
  [T3] ──wait...    [ conn3 ] (idle)       └────────┘
                    [ conn4 ] (idle)
```

## Key configuration parameters

| Setting | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Min / initial size** | Connections kept open even when idle |
| **Max size** | Upper limit; borrowers wait when all are in use |
| **Connection timeout** | How long a borrower waits before failing |
| **Idle timeout** | Close connections unused for this long |
| **Max lifetime** | Retire connections after a set age (avoids stale/leaked server-side state) |
| **Validation / test query** | Check a connection is alive before handing it out |

## Benefits

- **Lower latency** — no per-request handshake/auth cost
- **Bounded load** — the max size protects the database from being overwhelmed
- **Better throughput** — reuse means more queries per second
- **Predictable resource use** — connections are a finite, controlled resource

## Sizing and gotchas

- **Bigger is not better.** A common formula is roughly `connections ≈ ((core_count * 2) + effective_spindle_count)`. Too many connections cause context-switching and lock contention on the DB, often *reducing* throughput.
- **Connection leaks** — if code borrows but forgets to return (e.g., missing `finally`/`using`/context manager), the pool drains and everything stalls. Always release in a `finally` block or use language constructs that auto-return.
- **Serverless / many instances** — each app instance has its own pool, so `instances × max_size` can blow past the DB's limit. This is where an external pooler like **PgBouncer** (transaction-level pooling) helps.
- **Stale connections** — networks and databases drop idle connections; `max lifetime` and validation queries guard against handing out dead ones.

## Where it lives

- **In-process libraries**: HikariCP (Java), `database/sql` (Go), SQLAlchemy's `QueuePool` (Python), `pg-pool` (Node).
- **External/sidecar poolers**: PgBouncer, Pgpool-II (Postgres), ProxySQL (MySQL) — useful when many app instances share one database.

**In short:** connection pooling trades a small amount of memory (keeping connections open) for large savings in latency and stability by reusing a controlled set of connections instead of constantly creating and tearing them down.

Database connection pooling is a technique for managing and reusing database connections rather than opening a new one for every request.

## The problem it solves

Opening a database connection is expensive. Each one requires:
- A TCP handshake (and often a TLS handshake)
- Authentication (username/password, sometimes cert exchange)
- Server-side session/memory allocation

This can take tens to hundreds of milliseconds. If your app opens and closes a connection per query, that overhead dominates, and under load you can exhaust the database's connection limit and crash it.

## How pooling works

A pool is a cache of pre-established, open connections kept ready for reuse:

1. On startup, the pool opens a set of connections (or opens them lazily on first use).
2. When code needs the database, it **borrows** a connection from the pool.
3. It runs its queries.
4. Instead of closing, it **returns** the connection to the pool, which resets it and keeps it open for the next borrower.

```
Request ─► [ borrow ] ─► use connection ─► [ return ]
                            ▲                    │
                            └──── pool ◄─────────┘
                       (idle, reusable connections)
```

## Key configuration knobs

| Setting | What it controls |
|---|---|
| **Min / initial size** | Connections kept open even when idle |
| **Max size** | Ceiling on concurrent connections (protects the DB) |
| **Acquire / wait timeout** | How long a borrower waits when all connections are busy |
| **Idle timeout** | When to close connections that sit unused |
| **Max lifetime** | Retire connections after a while to avoid stale/leaked state |
| **Validation / test query** | Check a connection is still alive before handing it out |

## Behavior under load

- If all connections are in use, new requests **wait** in a queue until one frees up (or time out).
- **Max size** is the critical safety limit: it prevents your app from overwhelming the database. Total DB load ≈ (instances × pool max size), which matters a lot when you scale horizontally — 20 app servers × 50 connections = 1,000 connections hitting one database.

## Things to watch out for

- **Connection leaks** — borrowing without returning (forgetting to close/release) drains the pool until everything hangs. Always release in a `finally` block or use language constructs that auto-return (`with`, `try-with-resources`, `defer`).
- **Pool too small** — requests queue and latency spikes even though the DB is idle.
- **Pool too large** — you overwhelm the DB, and context-switching between many connections can actually *reduce* throughput.
- **Stale connections** — network blips or DB restarts can silently kill pooled connections; validation/max-lifetime settings mitigate this.

## Where it lives

- **Client-side libraries:** HikariCP (Java), `database/sql` (Go), SQLAlchemy (Python), `pg`/`node-postgres` pool, `.NET` built-in pooling.
- **External poolers:** PgBouncer, Pgpool-II (Postgres), ProxySQL (MySQL) — a separate process that pools on behalf of many app instances, useful for serverless/high-fan-out setups where per-instance pools would otherwise multiply.

## Rule of thumb for sizing

Bigger isn't better. A common starting formula is roughly `connections ≈ (2 × CPU cores) + effective spindle count` on the database, then tune with real metrics. Many high-traffic systems run well with surprisingly small pools (10–20 per instance).

**Database connection pooling** — reuse a fixed set of open DB connections instead of opening/closing one per request.

**Why:** TCP handshake + auth + TLS + backend process spawn = expensive (ms-scale, plus DB-side memory per connection). Under load, per-request connects exhaust DB max connections and dominate latency.

**Mechanism:** pool holds N live connections. Request → *borrow* (acquire) → run query → *return* (release) to pool, not close. Idle connections wait for the next borrower. Connection never actually closes under normal operation.

**Key knobs:**
- `min`/`max` size — floor kept warm, ceiling caps concurrent DB load.
- acquire timeout — borrower waits when all busy; exceed → error (backpressure signal, not a hang).
- idle timeout — reap surplus connections back down to `min`.
- max lifetime / recycle — retire old connections (avoids stale/leaked server-side state, honors DB-side timeouts).
- validation — ping/`SELECT 1` before handout to drop dead connections.

**Sizing:** not "bigger = faster". Optimal ≈ cores/active-queries on the DB, not app request count. Oversized pool → DB context-thrash + connection-limit exhaustion. Common formula start: `connections ≈ ((core_count × 2) + effective_spindle_count)`.

**Failure modes:**
- *Pool exhaustion* — leaked connections (borrowed, never released; missing `finally`/`with`) or slow queries hold the pool empty → all requests block on acquire.
- *Stale connection* — DB/firewall killed it server-side; without validation, first query fails. Fix: validation query or max-lifetime < server idle timeout.

**Where it lives:**
- In-process (HikariCP, SQLAlchemy `QueuePool`, `pg`/`node-postgres`) — one pool per app instance; M instances × max = total DB load.
- External proxy (PgBouncer, ProxySQL, RDS Proxy) — pools across many app instances; needed at high instance count. PgBouncer modes: *session* (per-connection), *transaction* (per-tx, highest reuse but breaks session state — prepared statements, `SET`, advisory locks), *statement*.

**Serverless caveat:** per-invocation processes can't hold a pool → each cold start opens a connection → exhaustion. Use an external proxy (RDS Proxy / PgBouncer) as the shared pool.

Database connection pooling: reuse a fixed set of open DB connections instead of opening/closing one per request.

**Problem it solves.** TCP handshake + TLS + auth + backend process spawn = ~ms–tens of ms per new connection. Under load, that cost dominates, and DBs cap concurrent connections (Postgres default 100). Open-per-request → latency + exhaustion.

**Mechanism.** Pool holds N live connections. Request lifecycle:
- **acquire** — borrow an idle connection (or wait if none free)
- **use** — run queries
- **release** — return to pool, connection stays open for the next borrower

**Key knobs.**
- `min` / `max` size — floor of warm connections, ceiling to protect the DB
- `acquire/wait timeout` — max wait for a free connection before erroring (fail fast vs. queue forever)
- `idle timeout` — close connections idle too long, shrink toward `min`
- `max lifetime` — recycle old connections (avoid stale/leaked server-side state)
- `validation` — ping/health-check before handing out (drop dead connections)

**Sizing.** More is not better. Optimal `max` ≈ cores × factor, not thousands — past the DB's parallelism, extra connections add contention. Total across all app instances must stay under the server limit: `instances × pool_max ≤ server max_connections`. Leave headroom for admin/migrations.

**Two layers.**
- *In-process* (HikariCP, `pg.Pool`, SQLAlchemy `QueuePool`) — pool lives in the app.
- *External proxy* (PgBouncer, RDS Proxy, ProxySQL) — shared pool in front of the DB, essential for serverless/many-instance deploys where each instance can't hold its own large pool. PgBouncer transaction-mode multiplexes many clients onto few server connections.

**Main failure modes.**
- *Leak* — borrow without release → pool drains → every request blocks on acquire. Always release in `finally`/context manager.
- *Undersized* — requests queue on acquire; looks like slow DB, actually pool starvation.
- *Oversized* — DB hits `max_connections`, rejects new backends; or too many active queries thrash the server.
- *Stale* — connection cut by network/DB-side idle kill; validation or `max lifetime` prevents handing out a dead socket.

Rule of thumb: reuse over reconnect, cap below the server limit, always release, recycle old connections.

# Database Connection Pooling

Connection pooling is a technique for managing database connections efficiently by **reusing a cached set of open connections** rather than opening and closing a new one for every request.

## The Problem It Solves

Establishing a database connection is expensive. Each new connection requires:
- A TCP handshake (and often a TLS handshake)
- Authentication/authorization
- Session and memory allocation on the database server

For a web app handling thousands of requests, opening a fresh connection per request adds significant latency and can overwhelm the database, which has a hard cap on concurrent connections.

## How It Works

A **pool** is maintained by the application (or a middleware layer):

1. On startup, the pool opens a set of connections and keeps them idle.
2. When code needs the database, it **borrows** ("acquires") a connection from the pool.
3. When done, it **returns** ("releases") the connection to the pool instead of closing it.
4. If all connections are busy, new requests **wait in a queue** (up to a timeout) or the pool opens more connections up to a max limit.

```
Requests ──▶ [ Pool: conn1 conn2 conn3 ... ] ──▶ Database
   (borrow, use, return — connections stay open)
```

## Key Configuration Knobs

| Setting | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **min / initial size** | Connections kept open even when idle |
| **max size** | Upper bound on total connections (protects the DB) |
| **acquire timeout** | How long a request waits for a free connection before erroring |
| **idle timeout** | Close connections unused for this long, shrinking the pool |
| **max lifetime** | Retire a connection after a set age (avoids stale/leaked server-side state) |
| **validation / health check** | Test a connection before handing it out (e.g., `SELECT 1`) |

## Sizing the Pool

Bigger isn't better. A common starting formula:

```
pool_size ≈ (core_count × 2) + effective_spindle_count
```

Too large a pool causes contention on the DB (context switching, lock waits) and can exhaust the server's connection limit. Too small causes request queuing. Size it to the database's capacity, and remember the total across **all app instances** must stay under the DB's `max_connections`.

## Common Pitfalls

- **Connection leaks** — borrowing without releasing (forgetting to close/return in error paths) slowly drains the pool until it deadlocks. Always release in a `finally` block or use language constructs that auto-return (context managers, `try-with-resources`).
- **Stale connections** — a connection the DB or a firewall silently killed; mitigated by validation queries and max-lifetime.
- **Pool exhaustion under load** — long-running queries hold connections; combine sensible timeouts with max-size limits.
- **Multiplying pools** — each service replica has its own pool; serverless/autoscaled environments can blow past `max_connections`, which is why external poolers exist.

Pool = fixed set of pre-opened DB connections, reused across requests instead of opened/closed per query.

**Why.** Opening a connection is expensive: TCP handshake, TLS, auth, backend process/thread spawn (Postgres forks a process per connection). Per-query open/close adds 1–50ms latency and caps throughput. A pool amortizes that cost — connections stay warm and get handed out.

**Mechanism.**
- Startup: pool opens N connections, holds them idle.
- Request needs DB → *acquire* a free connection (fast, no handshake).
- Query runs → connection *returned* to pool, not closed.
- All busy → caller *waits* up to a timeout, then errors.

**Key knobs.**
- `min`/`max` size — floor of idle connections, ceiling of concurrent ones.
- acquire/wait timeout — how long a caller blocks before failing.
- idle timeout — reap connections unused too long.
- max lifetime — recycle a connection after T to dodge stale server state / load-balancer drift.
- validation — test with `SELECT 1` (or equivalent) before handing out, to drop dead sockets.

**Sizing.** Bounded by the DB's own connection limit, not app desire. Postgres `max_connections` default ~100; each connection ≈ real RAM. Rough start: `connections ≈ cores × 2..4`. Oversizing hurts — more connections than the DB can schedule adds contention, not throughput.

**Multi-tier gotcha.** Per-instance pools multiply: 20 app instances × 50-max pool = 1000 connections at the DB. This is where a server-side pooler (PgBouncer, RDS Proxy, ProxySQL) sits between app and DB, multiplexing many client connections onto few server ones.

**Transaction pitfall.** Reusing connections leaks state — open transactions, session vars (`SET`), temp tables, prepared statements. Pool must reset or the next borrower inherits it. PgBouncer's `transaction` mode returns the connection at each commit; incompatible with session-level features.

Common pools: HikariCP (Java), pgbouncer (Postgres proxy), SQLAlchemy `QueuePool` (Python), `pg.Pool` (Node), `database/sql` built-in (Go).

**TCP** (Transmission Control Protocol) and **UDP** (User Datagram Protocol) are the two main transport-layer protocols. The core trade-off is **reliability vs. speed**.

## TCP — Connection-oriented, reliable

- **Handshake first:** Establishes a connection via a 3-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) before any data flows.
- **Guaranteed delivery:** Every packet is acknowledged; lost packets are retransmitted.
- **Ordered:** Data arrives in the sequence it was sent (packets are reassembled in order).
- **Flow & congestion control:** Adapts sending rate to the receiver's capacity and network conditions.
- **Higher overhead:** Larger headers (20+ bytes) and extra round-trips add latency.

## UDP — Connectionless, "fire and forget"

- **No handshake:** Just sends packets (datagrams) with no setup.
- **No delivery guarantee:** Lost packets are simply lost — no retransmission or acknowledgment.
- **No ordering:** Packets may arrive out of order.
- **No congestion control** (by default) — sends as fast as told.
- **Lower overhead:** Small 8-byte header, minimal latency.

## Quick comparison

| Feature | TCP | UDP |
|---|---|---|
| Connection | Required (handshake) | None |
| Reliability | Guaranteed | Best-effort |
| Ordering | Yes | No |
| Speed/latency | Slower | Faster |
| Header size | 20–60 bytes | 8 bytes |
| Flow/congestion control | Yes | No |

## When to use which

- **TCP:** Web (HTTP/HTTPS), email, file transfer (SSH, FTP) — anything where every byte must arrive correctly.
- **UDP:** Live video/voice (VoIP), gaming, DNS lookups, streaming — where speed matters more than perfect delivery, and a dropped packet is better than a delayed one.

**One nuance:** Modern protocols blur the line. QUIC (used by HTTP/3) is built *on top of* UDP but adds TCP-like reliability and ordering in user space, getting the best of both.

Database connection pooling is a technique for managing and reusing database connections rather than opening a new one for every request. Here's how it works and why it matters.

## The problem it solves

Opening a database connection is expensive. Each new connection involves:

- A TCP handshake (and often a TLS handshake)
- Authentication (username/password, credential validation)
- Backend resource allocation on the database server (memory, a process or thread)

This setup can take tens of milliseconds — often far longer than the actual query. If your app opens and closes a connection per request, that overhead dominates, and under load you can exhaust the database's connection limit entirely.

## How pooling works

A connection pool is a cache of open, ready-to-use connections maintained by your application (or a middleware layer).

```
1. On startup, the pool opens N connections and keeps them idle.
2. When code needs the DB, it "borrows" a connection from the pool.
3. It runs its queries.
4. Instead of closing, it "returns" the connection to the pool.
5. The next request reuses that same warm connection.
```

The connection is never really closed during normal operation — it's just lent out and reclaimed.

## Key configuration parameters

| Parameter | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|
| **Min pool size** | Connections kept open even when idle |
| **Max pool size** | Upper limit on concurrent connections |
| **Connection timeout** | How long a caller waits for a free connection before erroring |
| **Idle timeout** | How long an unused connection stays open before being closed |
| **Max lifetime** | Force-recycle a connection after this age (avoids stale/leaked server state) |

**Sizing the max is the most important decision.** Bigger is not better — each connection consumes resources on the database server. A common starting formula is roughly `connections = ((core_count * 2) + effective_spindle_count)`, but the real answer comes from load testing. Many small apps run fine with a pool of 10–20.

## What happens under saturation

When all connections are in use, new requests **queue** (up to the connection timeout) rather than opening unlimited connections. This is a feature: it applies backpressure and protects the database from being overwhelmed, trading some request latency for overall stability.

## Practical notes

- **Use an existing library.** HikariCP (Java), `pgbouncer` (Postgres, external), SQLAlchemy's pool (Python), `database/sql`'s built-in pool (Go), node-postgres `Pool` (Node). Don't build your own.
- **Serverless/edge is a special case.** Many short-lived function instances each holding a pool can blow past DB limits. Use an external pooler like PgBouncer (in transaction mode) or a data proxy (e.g., RDS Proxy, Prisma Accelerate) that pools centrally.
- **Always return connections** — a leaked connection (borrowed but never returned) permanently shrinks the pool. Use language constructs (`try/finally`, context managers, `defer`) to guarantee return.
- **Validate on borrow** if your network drops idle connections; a `maxLifetime` shorter than the DB/firewall idle timeout avoids handing out dead connections.

## The trade-off in one line

Pooling trades a small amount of memory and complexity for a large reduction in per-request latency and a hard cap that shields your database from connection exhaustion.

Connection pooling: reuse a fixed set of open DB connections instead of opening one per request.

**Why.** TCP handshake + TLS + DB auth + session setup = expensive (ms-scale, often dwarfs the query). Under load, open-per-request also exhausts the DB's connection cap (each connection = server memory + a backend process/thread).

**Mechanism.** Pool holds N live connections. Request flow:
1. Borrow (acquire/checkout) an idle connection — or wait if all busy.
2. Run query on it.
3. Return (release/check-in) to pool, still open, reset to a clean state.
Connection stays open across many requests → handshake cost paid once, amortized.

**Key knobs.**
- `min`/`max` size — floor kept warm, ceiling caps concurrent DB load.
- acquire timeout — how long a caller waits for a free connection before erroring (pool exhaustion).
- idle timeout — reap connections unused too long, shrink toward min.
- max lifetime — recycle old connections (avoid stale/leaked server-side state, respect DB-side timeouts).
- validation — ping/test-on-borrow to drop dead connections before handing out.

**Sizing.** Bigger ≠ faster. Optimal pool ≈ cores × small factor, not thousands — past the DB's parallelism, connections just queue and add contention. `pool_size × app_instances ≤ DB max_connections`; overshoot → DB refuses connections.

**Failure modes.**
- Leak: borrow without release → pool drains → every caller blocks on acquire. Always release in `finally`/context manager.
- Long-held connection (used across a slow external call, or an open transaction spanning I/O) → starves others.
- Sizing app pool larger than DB cap → connection-refused under load.

**Where it lives.** In-process library (HikariCP, `psycopg_pool`, SQLAlchemy `QueuePool`, Go `database/sql`) or an external proxy (PgBouncer, ProxySQL) that pools on behalf of many app instances — needed when instance count × per-instance pool would blow the DB cap. PgBouncer modes: session (connection per client session), transaction (per transaction — highest reuse, but breaks session-scoped features like prepared statements/`SET`), statement.

Serverless caveat: many short-lived processes each holding a pool = anti-pattern → use an external pooler or a data proxy.

**TCP** — connection-oriented, reliable.
- Handshake first (SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK) before data.
- Guarantees delivery + ordering; ACKs + retransmit lost segments.
- Flow control (receiver window) + congestion control.
- Byte stream (no message boundaries).
- Higher overhead/latency; 20-byte header.
- Use: HTTP(S), SSH, file transfer, email — anything where correctness > speed.

**UDP** — connectionless, best-effort.
- No handshake; fire packets immediately.
- No delivery/order guarantee; no retransmit (app handles if needed).
- No flow/congestion control.
- Datagrams (preserved message boundaries).
- Low overhead/latency; 8-byte header. Supports broadcast/multicast.
- Use: DNS, VoIP, video streaming, games, QUIC/HTTP3 — where speed/timeliness > perfect delivery.

Core tradeoff: TCP buys reliability with latency + state; UDP buys speed by pushing reliability (if wanted) up to the app.

| Header | 20+ bytes | 8 bytes |
| Speed/latency | slower — handshake + ACK overhead | faster — no setup, no waiting |
| Data model | byte stream | discrete datagrams |

**Use UDP:** DNS, VoIP, live video, games — where a late packet is worse than a lost one; app handles loss if needed.

Here's a practical playbook for tracking down and fixing a memory leak in a long-running Node.js process.

## 1. Confirm it's actually a leak

Watch memory over time under steady load. A leak shows a rising floor (heap used after GC keeps climbing); normal sawtooth that returns to a baseline is fine.

```js
setInterval(() => {
  const m = process.memoryUsage();
  console.log(JSON.stringify({
    rss: m.rss, heapUsed: m.heapUsed, external: m.external, arrayBuffers: m.arrayBuffers
  }));
}, 30_000);
```

- `heapUsed` growing → JS objects leaking.
- `external` / `arrayBuffers` growing → Buffers, typed arrays, or native addons.
- `rss` growing while heap is flat → native memory / fragmentation.

## 2. Capture heap snapshots and diff them

This is the highest-signal step. Take a snapshot, apply load, force GC, take another, and compare.

```bash
node --inspect --expose-gc your-app.js
```

Then in Chrome DevTools (`chrome://inspect`) → Memory → take snapshot 1, run traffic, take snapshot 2, and use the **Comparison** view to see which object types grew. Or programmatically:

```js
const v8 = require('v8');
const fs = require('fs');
// call twice, minutes apart, then diff in DevTools
fs.writeFileSync(`heap-${Date.now()}.heapsnapshot`, '');
const s = v8.getHeapSnapshot();
s.pipe(fs.createWriteStream(`heap-${Date.now()}.heapsnapshot`));
```

Load both `.heapsnapshot` files into DevTools. Look at **Retainers** to see what's holding the growing objects alive — that path points straight at the bug.

## 3. The usual suspects

Most leaks in long-running processes are one of these:

- **Unbounded caches / Maps / arrays** — you `.push()` or `map.set()` but never evict. Fix with an LRU (`lru-cache`) or a size/TTL bound. This is the #1 cause.
- **Event listeners never removed** — `emitter.on(...)` in a per-request path without `off`/`removeListener`. The `MaxListenersExceededWarning` is a tell. Use `once` where appropriate, or remove listeners on cleanup.
- **Timers / intervals** — `setInterval` that's never `clearInterval`'d, or timers holding closures over big objects.
- **Closures capturing large scopes** — a long-lived callback closing over a big buffer/request object keeps it all alive.
- **Global state** — appending to module-level arrays, or a growing `Map` keyed by user/session with no expiry. Consider `WeakMap`/`WeakRef` when keys are objects and you want GC to reclaim them.
- **Native/`external` leaks** — database driver, streams not destroyed, or a broken addon.

## 4. Add guardrails

- Raise heap headroom / set a hard limit so you get crashes (and heap dumps) instead of silent OOM kills: `--max-old-space-size=<MB>`.
- Auto-dump on OOM: `node --heapsnapshot-near-heap-limit=2 --max-old-space-size=2048 app.js`.
- In production, tools like `clinic.js` (`clinic heapprofiler`) or `node --prof` help when you can't attach a debugger.

## 5. Verify the fix

Re-run the same load test and confirm `heapUsed` returns to a stable baseline after GC across many cycles — not just that it grows slower.

## 1. Confirm it's actually a leak

Watch memory over time under steady load. A leak shows heap that climbs and never fully recovers after GC.

```js
setInterval(() => {
  const m = process.memoryUsage();
  console.log(JSON.stringify({
    rss: m.rss, heapUsed: m.heapUsed, external: m.external,
    arrayBuffers: m.arrayBuffers,
  }));
}, 10_000);
```

- **`heapUsed` climbing** → JS object leak (find it via heap snapshots).
- **`external` / `arrayBuffers` climbing** but heap flat → Buffers, or native addon memory.
- **`rss` climbing** but heap flat → native leak, or fragmentation.

Run with `node --expose-gc` and force `global.gc()` before sampling to rule out lazy collection.

## 2. Find what's retained (heap snapshots)

The most reliable technique is the **three-snapshot diff**:

1. Start the process, let it warm up, take a snapshot.
2. Drive the leaking workload for a while.
3. Take a second snapshot, more load, then a third.

```bash
node --inspect your-app.js
# open chrome://inspect → Memory → take snapshots
```

In DevTools, use the **Comparison** view between snapshots and sort by "Delta". Objects that keep growing across all three are your leak. Click one and read the **Retainers** tree — that shows the chain of references keeping it alive, which points straight at the offending code.

For headless/production capture:

```js
const v8 = require('v8');
process.on('SIGUSR2', () => v8.writeHeapSnapshot()); // kill -USR2 <pid>
```

Load the `.heapsnapshot` files into Chrome DevTools afterward. `clinic heapprofiler` and `heapdump` are also useful.

## 3. Common causes and fixes

| Cause | Fix |
|---|---|
| **Unbounded module-level `Map`/`Array`/cache** growing forever | Cap size, use `lru-cache`, or `WeakMap`/`WeakRef` so entries can be GC'd |
| **Event listeners added, never removed** (esp. per-request `.on()`) | `removeListener`/`off`, use `{ once: true }`, watch for `MaxListenersExceededWarning` |
| **Timers / intervals never cleared** | `clearInterval`/`clearTimeout`; use `.unref()` where appropriate |
| **Closures capturing large objects** held by long-lived callbacks | Null out references, narrow closure scope |
| **Global/singleton accumulation** (logs, request history, metrics) | Bound the collection, flush/rotate |
| **Promises/streams never settling** | Ensure they resolve/reject; destroy streams |
| **Native addons / Buffers** | Update the addon; pool or explicitly release Buffers |

- Set a hard limit so leaks fail fast instead of thrashing: `node --max-old-space-size=2048`.
- Track GC pressure with `--trace-gc`.
- Add a memory-usage alert in production so you catch regressions early.

The SQL `EXPLAIN` command shows you the **query execution plan** — how the database engine intends to run your query *before* (or instead of) actually running it. It's the primary tool for diagnosing why a query is slow.

## What it tells you

**1. Access method — how each table is read**
- Full table scan (reads every row) vs. index scan/seek (uses an index)
- Which index, if any, was chosen
- This is usually the biggest performance signal: a full scan on a large table is a common culprit for slow queries.

**2. Join strategy — how tables are combined**
- Nested loop, hash join, or merge join
- The order in which tables are joined

**3. Cost & row estimates**
- The optimizer's *estimated* cost (relative units)
- Estimated number of rows at each step
- When estimates are wildly off from reality, it often means stale statistics.

**4. Extra operations**
- Sorts, aggregations, temporary tables, filtering steps

## `EXPLAIN` vs `EXPLAIN ANALYZE`
- **`EXPLAIN`** — shows the *estimated* plan without running the query.
- **`EXPLAIN ANALYZE`** — *actually executes* the query and shows real timing and actual row counts alongside the estimates. This lets you spot where estimates diverge from reality. ⚠️ Because it runs the query, be careful with `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE` — wrap those in a transaction you can roll back.

## What to look for
- Full/sequential scans on large tables → consider adding an index
- Big gaps between estimated and actual rows → update table statistics
- Unexpected sorts or temp tables → may indicate missing indexes or rewritable queries

## A note on syntax
Details vary by database:
- **PostgreSQL** — `EXPLAIN`, `EXPLAIN ANALYZE`, plus `FORMAT JSON` and options like `BUFFERS`
- **MySQL** — `EXPLAIN`, `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` (8.0.18+), `EXPLAIN FORMAT=JSON`
- **SQL Server** — uses execution plans (`SET SHOWPLAN_ALL ON` / graphical plans) rather than `EXPLAIN`
- **Oracle** — `EXPLAIN PLAN FOR ...` then query `DBMS_XPLAN`
- **SQLite** — `EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN`

- Full table scan vs. index scan/seek
- Which index (if any) is used

- Relative cost of each operation

The SQL `EXPLAIN` command shows you the **query execution plan** — how the database engine intends to run your query, *without actually running it* (in most cases). It's the primary tool for diagnosing why a query is slow.

## What it tells you

**Access method** — how each table is read:
- Full table scan (reads every row — often the culprit for slow queries)
- Index scan / index seek (uses an index)
- Which specific index was chosen

**Join strategy** — for multi-table queries, the algorithm and order:
- Nested loop, hash join, merge join
- Which table is scanned first (the "driving" table)

**Cost & row estimates** — the optimizer's guesses:
- Estimated number of rows processed at each step
- Relative cost numbers used to compare plans
- These are *estimates* from table statistics, which is important (see below)

**Extra operations** — sorts, temporary tables, filesort, aggregations, etc.

## Reading it — a Postgres example

```sql
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 42;
```
```
Seq Scan on orders  (cost=0.00..1834.00 rows=12 width=44)
  Filter: (customer_id = 42)
```
`Seq Scan` = full table scan. If `orders` is large and you query `customer_id` often, that signals a missing index.

## Key variations

| Command | What it adds |
|---|---|
| `EXPLAIN` | The estimated plan only (doesn't run the query) |
| `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` | **Actually runs it** and shows *real* timings and row counts |
| `EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)` | Postgres — adds actual disk/cache I/O |
| `EXPLAIN FORMAT=JSON` | MySQL — detailed structured output |

Compare **estimated rows vs. actual rows** using `EXPLAIN ANALYZE`. A large mismatch means the optimizer's statistics are stale — run `ANALYZE` (Postgres) or `ANALYZE TABLE` (MySQL) to refresh them, which often fixes a bad plan on its own.

⚠️ `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` **executes the query**, including `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE`. Wrap those in a transaction and roll back if you don't want the changes to persist.

Not a code task — no repo to inspect. Here's the workflow.

## 1. Confirm it's actually a leak
Log heap over time: `process.memoryUsage().heapUsed`. Real leak = monotonic rise across GC cycles that never recovers. Sawtooth that returns to baseline = normal. Run with `--expose-gc` and force `global.gc()` before sampling to rule out lazy GC.

## 2. Capture heap snapshots
Two ways:
- `node --inspect app.js` → Chrome DevTools → Memory → take snapshot at T0, run load, snapshot at T1, **compare**. Sort by "Delta". Growing object counts = your leak.
- Programmatic: `require('v8').writeHeapSnapshot()` at intervals, diff in DevTools.

Take ≥3 snapshots; the retained set that grows every time is the culprit. Check the **retainer chain** (what holds the object alive) — that points straight at the offending reference.

## 3. Common causes, in order of frequency
- **Unbounded growth of module-scope collections** — a global `Map`/`Array`/cache you push to but never evict. Fix: bound it (LRU, e.g. `lru-cache`), or use `WeakMap`/`WeakRef` so entries GC when keys die.
- **Listeners never removed** — `emitter.on(...)` in a per-request/per-connection path without `removeListener`/`off`. Symptom: `MaxListenersExceededWarning`. Fix: `once`, or remove on cleanup.
- **Timers holding closures** — `setInterval`/`setTimeout` never cleared; the closure retains everything it captured. Fix: `clearInterval` on teardown.
- **Closures capturing large scope** — a long-lived callback closes over a big buffer/object. Fix: null the reference or narrow the closure.
- **Dangling references in caches keyed by request/session** — grows with traffic. Same fix: bounded/weak.
- Detached DOM-equivalent: promises that never settle, keeping `.then` chains alive.

## 4. Fix and prove it
Apply the eviction/removal, then **soak test**: drive sustained load (autocannon/k6) and watch `heapUsed` — must plateau, not climb. That's the verification; typecheck/lint proves nothing here.

## Tooling shortcuts
- `clinic doctor` / `clinic heapprofiler` — auto-diagnoses, generates flamegraphs.
- `node --heap-prof` — heap allocation profile without DevTools.
- `--max-old-space-size` only raises the ceiling; it hides, doesn't fix.

Most common single answer: **an unbounded cache or an un-removed event listener on a hot path.** Snapshot-diff to find which; bound or remove to fix; soak to prove.

The SQL `EXPLAIN` command shows you the **query execution plan** — how the database engine intends to run your query, without (usually) actually running it. It's the primary tool for understanding and diagnosing query performance.

## What it tells you

**Access method** — how each table is read:
- Full table scan (reads every row — often slow on big tables)
- Index scan / index seek (uses an index)
- Which specific index was chosen

**Join strategy** — how multiple tables are combined:
- Nested loop, hash join, merge join, etc.
- The **order** tables are joined in

**Cost & row estimates:**
- Estimated number of rows processed at each step
- The optimizer's relative cost estimate
- Sometimes width (bytes per row) and startup vs. total cost

**Operations** — sorts, aggregations, filters, temporary tables, and whether they happen in memory or spill to disk.

## Why it matters

You use it to answer questions like:
- Is my query using the index I created, or ignoring it?
- Why is this query slow — is it scanning millions of rows?
- Is a join blowing up row counts unexpectedly?

## Estimated vs. actual

Plain `EXPLAIN` shows the optimizer's **estimates**. Most engines have a variant that runs the query and reports **real** numbers, which is far more useful for spotting bad estimates:

- **PostgreSQL:** `EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT ...` (actually executes it — careful with writes; wrap in a transaction and roll back if needed)
- **MySQL:** `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` (8.0+), or `EXPLAIN FORMAT=JSON` for detail
- **SQLite:** `EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN` (the plain `EXPLAIN` there dumps low-level bytecode instead)
- **SQL Server:** the equivalent is the execution plan (`SET SHOWPLAN_ALL ON` / graphical plan)

The key skill is reading it: look for full scans on large tables, big gaps between estimated and actual row counts, and expensive sorts — those are your optimization targets.

The SQL `EXPLAIN` command shows you the **query execution plan** — how the database engine intends to run your query, without (usually) actually running it. It's your primary tool for understanding and diagnosing query performance.

## What it tells you

- **Access method** — whether the engine does a full table scan (reads every row) or an index scan/seek (uses an index). Full scans on big tables are a common red flag.
- **Index usage** — which indexes, if any, are being used. Often reveals that an index you expected to help is being ignored.
- **Join strategy** — how tables are joined: nested loop, hash join, or merge join, and in what order.
- **Row estimates** — how many rows the planner *thinks* each step will process. If estimates are wildly off from reality, your statistics are likely stale.
- **Cost estimates** — a relative number the optimizer uses to compare plans (arbitrary units, not milliseconds).
- **Operation order** — the tree of steps (sorts, filters, aggregations) and how data flows between them.

## EXPLAIN vs. EXPLAIN ANALYZE

- `EXPLAIN` — shows the *estimated* plan only; doesn't execute the query.
- `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` — actually **runs** the query and shows *real* timings and *actual* row counts alongside the estimates. This is the most useful form for tuning, because you can compare estimated vs. actual rows to spot bad optimizer decisions. ⚠️ Caution: it really executes, so be careful with `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE` (wrap in a transaction and roll back if needed).

## Dialect notes

- **PostgreSQL** — rich tree output; supports `EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT JSON)` for I/O and format control.
- **MySQL/MariaDB** — tabular output; `EXPLAIN FORMAT=JSON` and `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` (8.0.18+) give more detail.
- **SQLite** — `EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN` is the human-readable version (plain `EXPLAIN` dumps bytecode).
- **SQL Server** — equivalent is `SET SHOWPLAN_XML ON` or the "Actual/Estimated Execution Plan" in SSMS.

## What people typically look for

1. Sequential/full scans where an index seek was expected.
2. Large gaps between estimated and actual row counts (→ run `ANALYZE`/update statistics).
3. Expensive sorts or hash operations spilling to disk.
4. Join order and type that could be improved with an index.

A hash table handles collisions — cases where two keys hash to the same bucket index — using one of two main strategies:

## 1. Separate Chaining
Each bucket holds a secondary data structure (usually a linked list, sometimes a dynamic array or balanced tree) containing all entries that hashed there.

- **Insert:** compute the index, append/prepend the key-value pair to that bucket's list.
- **Lookup:** compute the index, then scan the list comparing keys.
- **Delete:** find and unlink the entry from the list.

If one bucket gets very long, some implementations (e.g. Java's `HashMap`) convert its list into a balanced tree so worst-case lookup for that bucket drops from O(n) to O(log n).

## 2. Open Addressing
All entries live directly in the array. On a collision, you *probe* for another open slot according to a fixed sequence:

- **Linear probing:** try index+1, index+2, … (cache-friendly, but suffers from *primary clustering*).
- **Quadratic probing:** try index+1², index+2², … (reduces clustering).
- **Double hashing:** step size is determined by a second hash function (best distribution).

Lookup follows the same probe sequence until it finds the key or an empty slot. Deletion needs care — you can't just empty a slot or you'd break probe chains, so entries are marked with a *tombstone*.

## Keeping collisions rare
Both approaches degrade as the table fills, measured by the **load factor** (entries ÷ buckets). When it crosses a threshold (commonly ~0.75), the table **resizes** — allocates a larger array and rehashes every element — to keep the average number of collisions low and operations at amortized **O(1)**.

| | Separate chaining | Open addressing |
|---|---|---|
| Storage | Pointers + external nodes | All in one array |
| Cache locality | Worse | Better |
| Max load factor | Can exceed 1.0 | Must stay < 1.0 |
| Deletion | Simple | Needs tombstones |

**In short:** collisions are inevitable (pigeonhole principle), so hash tables either *chain* colliding entries together in each bucket or *probe* for alternative slots — and resize to keep collisions infrequent.

Hash tables handle collisions — when two keys hash to the same bucket index — using two main families of strategies:

## 1. Separate Chaining
Each bucket holds a *secondary data structure* (usually a linked list) of all entries that hash there.

- **Insert:** append/prepend to the bucket's list.
- **Lookup:** hash to the bucket, then scan the list comparing keys.
- **Delete:** unlink the node from the list.

Simple, degrades gracefully under high load. Downside: extra memory for pointers and poor cache locality. Some implementations upgrade a bucket's list to a balanced tree once it gets long — Java's `HashMap` switches to a red-black tree past ~8 entries, bounding worst-case lookup at O(log n) instead of O(n).

## 2. Open Addressing
All entries live *in the array itself*. On collision, probe for another open slot using a defined sequence:

| Scheme | Probe sequence | Trade-off |
|--------|---------------|-----------|
| **Linear probing** | `h+1, h+2, …` | Cache-friendly, but *primary clustering* (long runs form) |
| **Quadratic probing** | `h+1, h+4, h+9, …` | Reduces clustering, but *secondary clustering* |
| **Double hashing** | `h + i·h₂(key)` | Best distribution, less cache-friendly |

Lookup follows the same probe sequence until it finds the key or an empty slot. **Deletion is tricky** — you can't just empty a slot (it would break probe chains for later keys), so you use a *tombstone* marker or backward-shift the cluster.

## Key supporting concept: Load Factor
Both approaches depend on the **load factor** α = n/m (entries ÷ buckets). As it rises, collisions rise. Tables **resize and rehash** (typically doubling capacity) when α crosses a threshold — ~0.75 for chaining, and lower (~0.5) for open addressing since it degrades faster as the table fills.

A newer variant worth knowing: **Robin Hood hashing** (an open-addressing tweak) evens out probe distances by having "richer" entries yield slots to "poorer" ones, tightening worst-case behavior.

## 1. Separate Chaining

Each bucket holds a secondary data structure (usually a linked list) of all entries that hashed to that index.

```
index 3 → [ ("cat", 1) ] → [ ("dog", 7) ] → null
```

- **Insert:** compute index, append to that bucket's list.
- **Lookup:** compute index, then scan the list comparing keys.
- **Delete:** unlink the node from the list.

| Scheme | Probe sequence | Notes |
|--------|---------------|-------|
| **Linear probing** | `h, h+1, h+2, …` | Fast, cache-friendly; suffers *primary clustering* |
| **Quadratic probing** | `h, h+1², h+2², …` | Reduces clustering; needs care to cover all slots |
| **Double hashing** | `h, h+g, h+2g, …` where `g` is a second hash | Best distribution, minimal clustering |

- **Lookup:** follow the same probe sequence until you find the key or an empty slot.
- **Delete:** you can't just empty the slot (it would break probe chains) — use a *tombstone* marker or backward-shift the cluster.

- **Robin Hood hashing** and **cuckoo hashing** are advanced open-addressing variants that bound worst-case probe length.

## 1. Separate Chaining

Each bucket holds a **pointer to a data structure** (usually a linked list) of all entries that hash there.

```
Index 0 → [ ]
Index 1 → [ "cat" → "dog" → "bird" ]   ← 3 keys collided here
Index 2 → [ "fish" ]
```

- **Insert:** append/prepend to the bucket's list.
- **Lookup:** hash to the bucket, then scan the list comparing keys.

## 2. Open Addressing

All entries live **directly in the bucket array**. On collision, you *probe* for another open slot using a defined sequence:

| Scheme | Probe sequence | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear probing** | try `i+1, i+2, ...` | Cache-friendly, but suffers *primary clustering* |
| **Quadratic probing** | try `i+1², i+2², ...` | Reduces clustering, needs careful table sizing |
| **Double hashing** | step by a second hash `h₂(key)` | Best distribution, less cache-friendly |

- **Lookup:** follow the same probe sequence until you find the key or an empty slot.
- **Deletion is tricky:** you can't just empty a slot (it would break probe chains), so you mark it with a *tombstone* or rehash the cluster.

## Key Supporting Concepts

- **Load factor** (α = entries / buckets): as it rises, collisions increase. Tables **resize and rehash** (e.g., double capacity) when α crosses a threshold — ~0.75 for chaining, lower (~0.5–0.7) for open addressing.
- **Good hash function:** distributing keys uniformly is the first line of defense — it minimizes collisions before any resolution strategy is even needed.

## Quick Comparison

| | Chaining | Open Addressing |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Extra pointers | Compact, in-array |
| Cache performance | Worse (pointer chasing) | Better (contiguous) |
| Load factor allowed | Can exceed 1.0 | Must stay < 1.0 |
| Deletion | Simple | Needs tombstones |

**In short:** collisions are resolved either by *storing multiple items per bucket* (chaining) or by *finding a different bucket* (open addressing), backed by a good hash function and resizing to keep collisions rare.

CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) errors happen when your browser blocks a request because the **server** at the target origin didn't return the headers the browser requires to allow a page from a *different* origin to read the response. It's a browser-enforced security rule — the request often actually reaches the server; the browser just refuses to hand the response back to your JS.

## What counts as "cross-origin"
Any difference in **scheme + host + port** between the page and the request target:
- `http://localhost:3000` → `http://localhost:8000` (different port) ✅ cross-origin
- `https://app.com` → `http://app.com` (different scheme) ✅ cross-origin
- `https://app.com` → `https://api.app.com` (different subdomain) ✅ cross-origin

## Most common causes
1. **Server doesn't send `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`** — the #1 cause. The fix is on the *server*, not the client.
2. **Preflight failing** — for non-simple requests (custom headers, `PUT`/`DELETE`, `Content-Type: application/json`), the browser first sends an `OPTIONS` request. If the server doesn't answer it with the right `Access-Control-Allow-Methods` / `Access-Control-Allow-Headers`, the real request never fires.
3. **Credentials mismatch** — using `credentials: 'include'` (cookies/auth) requires `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true` **and** a specific origin (you can't use `*` with credentials).

## The actual message matters
The exact console text tells you which case you're in, e.g.:
- *"No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header is present"* → cause #1
- *"Response to preflight request doesn't pass"* → cause #2
- *"...must not be the wildcard '*' when credentials mode is 'include'"* → cause #3

- **Load factor** α = entries/buckets. Rising α → more collisions. Exceed threshold (e.g. 0.75) → **resize + rehash** into a bigger table.
- Open addressing needs α < 1 always; chaining tolerates α > 1.
- Good hash function spreading keys uniformly is the first defense.

Which does what: chaining = simpler, handles high load, extra pointer memory; open addressing = better cache locality, no pointers, sensitive to load factor and clustering.

Two main strategies:

**Separate chaining** — each bucket holds a secondary structure (linked list, or a tree once long). Collision → append to that bucket's list. Lookup: hash to bucket, scan the chain comparing keys. Simple, tolerates high load factor, but pointer-chasing hurts cache.

**Open addressing** — all entries live in the array itself; on collision, probe for another slot by a fixed sequence:
- *Linear probing*: try i+1, i+2, … Cache-friendly, but clustering.
- *Quadratic probing*: i+1², i+2², … breaks up clustering.
- *Double hashing*: step size = a second hash of the key; best distribution.

Lookup follows the same probe sequence until it finds the key or an empty slot. Deletion needs tombstones (a "was here" marker) so probes don't stop early.

Cross-cutting:
- **Load factor** α = entries/buckets drives collision rate. Exceed threshold (chaining ~1.0, open addressing ~0.7) → **resize + rehash** all entries into a larger table.
- **Hash quality** matters — a poor hash clusters keys and degrades O(1) average toward O(n).

Real-world: Java `HashMap` = chaining, converts a bucket to a red-black tree past 8 entries (O(log n) worst case); Python `dict` and Go maps = open addressing variants.

CORS errors happen when your browser blocks a request because the server didn't explicitly say your frontend's origin is allowed to talk to it. It's a browser-enforced security rule (the server still receives the request — the browser just hides the response from your JS).

## The usual causes

1. **Server doesn't send the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header** — the most common one. The API needs to return a header naming your frontend's origin (e.g. `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:3000`) or `*`.

2. **Origin mismatch** — your page is on `http://localhost:3000` but the server allows `http://localhost:3001`, or `https` vs `http`, or `localhost` vs `127.0.0.1`. Origins must match exactly (scheme + host + port).

3. **Preflight (`OPTIONS`) failing** — for requests with custom headers (like `Authorization`), a non-simple method (`PUT`/`DELETE`), or `Content-Type: application/json`, the browser first sends an `OPTIONS` request. If the server doesn't handle it with the right `Access-Control-Allow-Methods` / `Access-Control-Allow-Headers`, the real request never fires.

4. **Credentials + wildcard** — if you send cookies (`credentials: 'include'`), the server **cannot** use `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`. It must echo the specific origin and also send `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`.

The exact browser message tells you which case you're in — e.g. *"No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header is present"* (case 1) vs *"...does not pass access control check: It does not have HTTP ok status"* (case 3, preflight).

CORS errors come from a browser security mechanism, not from a bug in your code per se. Here's what's actually happening and how to fix it.

## What the error means

Your frontend (say `http://localhost:3000`) is making a request to a different **origin** (say `http://localhost:8080` or `https://api.example.com`). The browser blocks the response unless the server explicitly says "this origin is allowed" via `Access-Control-Allow-*` headers. An origin = scheme + host + port; if any of those three differ, it's cross-origin.

Key point: **CORS is enforced by the browser, and it's the server's job to send the right headers.** You almost always fix this on the backend, not the frontend.

## Common causes & fixes

**1. Server doesn't send the allow header**
The fix depends on your backend. For example:
```js
// Express
app.use(cors({ origin: 'http://localhost:3000', credentials: true }));
```

**2. Preflight (OPTIONS) failing**
Non-simple requests (custom headers, `PUT`/`DELETE`, JSON content-type in some cases) trigger a preflight `OPTIONS` request first. The server must respond to `OPTIONS` with `200/204` and the appropriate `Access-Control-Allow-Methods` / `Access-Control-Allow-Headers`.

**3. Credentials + wildcard**
If you send cookies/auth (`credentials: 'include'`), the server **cannot** use `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`. It must echo the specific origin and also send `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`.

**4. Dev-only shortcut: a proxy**
In development, route API calls through your dev server so they're same-origin (e.g. Vite `server.proxy`, CRA `"proxy"` field, or Next.js rewrites). This sidesteps CORS entirely for local work.

- Your **backend framework** and the API URL

CORS errors happen when your browser blocks a request because the server didn't return headers explicitly permitting your page's origin to read the response. It's a browser-enforced security policy (the request often *does* reach the server — the browser just refuses to hand the response to your JS).

## What the error is actually telling you

Look at the exact wording in the console — it points to the fix:

- **"No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header is present"** → the server isn't sending CORS headers at all. Most common cause.
- **"...does not match the supplied origin"** → server allows a *different* origin than your page's.
- **"...response to preflight request doesn't pass"** → the `OPTIONS` preflight failed (see below).
- **"...credentials mode is 'include'"** → you're sending cookies/auth, which has stricter rules.

## The usual causes

1. **Frontend and backend on different origins** — e.g. app on `localhost:3000`, API on `localhost:8000`. Different port = different origin = CORS applies.
2. **Server doesn't set CORS headers.** The fix belongs on the **server**, not the client. You can't fix CORS from frontend code alone.
3. **Preflight (`OPTIONS`) requests.** Anything beyond a "simple" request (custom headers like `Authorization`, `Content-Type: application/json`, methods like `PUT`/`DELETE`) triggers a preflight `OPTIONS` call first. Your server must respond to `OPTIONS` with the right `Access-Control-Allow-*` headers.
4. **Credentials + wildcard.** If you send cookies (`credentials: 'include'`), the server **cannot** use `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` — it must echo the specific origin and also send `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`.

## How to fix (server side)

The server needs to send, for allowed requests:
```
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://your-frontend.example   (or the specific origin)
Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS
Access-Control-Allow-Headers: Content-Type, Authorization
```
Most frameworks have a middleware for this (Express: `cors`, FastAPI: `CORSMiddleware`, Spring: `@CrossOrigin`, etc.).

**In local dev**, the common alternative is a **proxy** so requests are same-origin (e.g. Vite/CRA `proxy` config, or Next.js rewrites) — no CORS headers needed.

A debouncer on a search input solves a specific problem: **you don't want to react to every single keystroke**.

Without debouncing, if a user types "laptop", you'd fire off work on every intermediate state — `l`, `la`, `lap`, `lapt`, `lapto`, `laptop` — six times. A debouncer waits until the user *pauses* typing (say, 300ms of no new input) before actually running the handler, so you only act on `laptop`.

## What it actually buys you

**Fewer network requests.** This is the big one for search. Each keystroke often triggers an API call to fetch suggestions or results. Debouncing collapses a burst of keystrokes into a single request once the user stops. That's less load on your backend, fewer rate-limit issues, and lower cost.

**Avoiding wasted / stale work.** The results for `lap` are irrelevant the moment the user types `t`. Without debouncing you compute and possibly render results nobody will ever look at. Worse, responses can arrive out of order — the `lap` response might land *after* the `laptop` response and overwrite it with stale data (a race condition). Firing fewer requests reduces this.

**Smoother UI.** Re-rendering a results list on every keystroke can cause flicker and jank, especially with large result sets or expensive filtering.

## The tradeoff

Debouncing adds **latency** — results feel slightly delayed because you're deliberately waiting for a pause. The delay is a tuning knob: too short (~100ms) and you barely save any requests; too long (~800ms) and it feels sluggish. Somewhere around **250–400ms** is typical for search.

## Debounce vs. throttle

Worth knowing the distinction, since people mix them up:
- **Debounce** — wait until activity *stops*, then fire once. Ideal for search input (you care about the final value).
- **Throttle** — fire at most once per interval *during* activity. Better for things like scroll or resize handlers where you want periodic updates, not just the end state.

For a search box, debounce is almost always what you want — the intermediate keystrokes have no value, only the moment the user settles on a query.

One caveat: debouncing is a UX optimization, **not a correctness guarantee**. You still want request cancellation (e.g. `AbortController`) or response-ordering checks to fully defend against stale results, since a debounced request can still be superseded if the user resumes typing after it fires.

Mechanism: browser sends `Origin: https://yoursite.com`. For it to succeed, the response needs `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` matching that origin (or `*`). Non-simple requests (custom headers, `PUT`/`DELETE`, JSON content-type) first send an `OPTIONS` preflight — server must answer that with the allow headers too.

Read the exact console line — it names the cause:

- `No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header` → server sends nothing. Add the header server-side.
- `does not match` → header present but wrong origin. Echo the request origin or list it.
- `preflight ... 404/405` → `OPTIONS` route not handled. Handle OPTIONS.
- `header ... not allowed by Access-Control-Allow-Headers` → add that header (e.g. `Authorization`, `Content-Type`) to the allow list.
- `credentials mode` / cookies → set `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true` **and** a specific origin (`*` is illegal with credentials); client sets `credentials: 'include'`.

Fix is server-side, e.g. Express: `app.use(cors({ origin: 'https://yoursite.com', credentials: true }))`. Can't touch the server (third-party API) → proxy through your own backend; browsers don't enforce CORS on server-to-server calls.

A debouncer on a search input delays running the "expensive" work until the user *stops* typing for a short interval (say 300ms). Without it, every keystroke fires immediately. Here's why that matters:

## The core problems it solves

**1. Fewer wasted network requests**
If someone types "keyboard", an un-debounced input fires a request for `k`, `ke`, `key`, `keyb`, … — 8 requests, 7 of which are instantly obsolete. Debouncing collapses that into a single request for `keyboard` once they pause. That's less load on your backend, fewer database queries, and less bandwidth.

**2. Avoiding out-of-order results (race conditions)**
Those rapid requests can come back in any order. The response for `key` might arrive *after* the response for `keyboard`, leaving the user staring at results for a stale query. Fewer in-flight requests means fewer chances for this. (Note: debouncing reduces this but doesn't fully eliminate it — you still want request cancellation or a "latest wins" guard for correctness.)

**3. Less UI churn / re-rendering**
Even for purely local/client-side filtering over a big list, recomputing and re-rendering on every keystroke can cause jank. Debouncing lets the browser keep up with typing smoothly.

**4. Cost**
If your search hits a paid API (e.g. a search service, geocoding, or an LLM), each keystroke-request costs money. Debouncing can cut that by ~80–90%.

## The tradeoff
The cost is a small perceived latency — results appear a beat after the user stops typing. You tune the delay to balance responsiveness against savings; 200–400ms is typical.

## A note on debounce vs. throttle
- **Debounce**: wait until activity *stops*, then fire once. Best for search-as-you-type, where you only care about the final value.
- **Throttle**: fire at most once every N ms *during* continuous activity. Better for things like scroll or resize handlers where you want periodic updates throughout.

For a search box, debounce is almost always the right choice because the intermediate values ("k", "ke") have no standalone value — you only care where the user lands.

Both integrate changes from one branch into another, but they do it differently:

## `git merge`
Takes the commits from the source branch and combines them into the target branch with a **merge commit** that ties the two histories together.

```
      A---B---C  (feature)
     /         \
D---E---F---G---M  (main)
```

Moves (replays) your commits on top of the tip of another branch, **rewriting them as new commits** with new hashes.

# Git Rebase vs Git Merge

Both integrate changes from one branch into another, but they do it differently and produce different history.

## Merge

Combines branches by creating a **new merge commit** that ties the two histories together.

```
      A---B---C  feature
     /         \
D---E---F---G---H  main   ← H is the merge commit
```

- **Non-destructive** — existing commits are never changed.
- History shows *exactly what happened*, including when branches diverged and rejoined.
- Can produce a cluttered, hard-to-follow log with many merge commits.
- Conflicts are resolved once, in the single merge commit.

```bash
git checkout main
git merge feature
```

## Rebase

**Replays** your branch's commits on top of another branch, one by one, creating **new commits** with new hashes.

```
              A'--B'--C'  feature
             /
D---E---F---G  main
```

- **Rewrites history** — the original A, B, C are replaced by new commits A', B', C'.
- Produces a **linear, clean history** with no merge commits.
- Conflicts may need to be resolved *per commit* during the replay.
- Loses the record of when the branch actually diverged.

```bash
git checkout feature
git rebase main
```

## Key differences at a glance

| | Merge | Rebase |
|---|---|---|
| History | Preserved, non-linear | Rewritten, linear |
| New commits | One merge commit | Rewrites each commit |
| Traceability | Shows true branch topology | Cleaner but "invented" history |
| Conflict resolution | Once | Potentially per commit |
| Safety | Safe on shared branches | Risky on shared branches |

## The golden rule

**Never rebase commits that have been pushed to a shared branch** others are working on. Rebasing rewrites commit hashes, so anyone who has the old commits will get a divergent history and painful conflicts. Rebase private/local work; merge shared work.

## Practical guidance

- **Rebase** your local feature branch onto the latest `main` before opening a PR to keep history tidy: `git rebase main`.
- **Merge** to bring a completed feature into `main`, preserving the context that it was a distinct piece of work.
- A common hybrid: rebase locally to clean up, then merge with `--no-ff` to record the integration point.
